As the interviewer I did not see the 'hint' and 'solution' options in my screen - I was in observer mode and only saw what the candidate did. I'd looked prior but if any interviewer was on the hop they would fly blind. The other interviewer didn't see it either - and he set it up.

Hacker rank needs a button called "Request to lookup syntax" with a reason box  to provide a way for them to tab out without being marked a cheating when interviewers give consent. Possibly HackerRank could itself use AI to answer the question mediating the information provided.

Really common sticking point is: candidate wants to call sort library and can't figure out the syntax to pass a lambda in as the sort condition. As developers get this from the IDE they never know this - in a 'text editor' interface they invariably need to lookup.  I note as interviewers we can try and find out for the candidate - but in a language we don't know (python3 was selected - I've only done golang & java). Workaround is we hit ChatGPT on their behalf but extended delays wastes precious time and having established they don't know the syntax doesn't given additional information.  

Overall interview worked well and we got a good insight about the developer's level. But we pointless wasted a lot of time when they got stuck - would be good to make that more seamless - especially as it's always the same syntax edge-case: how to call sort - which IDEs do on developer's behalf in real life - so no-one knows it. The workaround for us is to get that python3 syntax and be ready to feed it to the candiate - but now sure in the UI how we do that - perhaps there is a chat window in the UI (sorry, didn't look). If we use the teams meeting they will tab away and alert anti-cheat controls.

Making "I'm stuck on something stupid" -> small help -> move on more seamless without tab-out by the candidate would be a a great bonus - trying to tell them a line of code verbally is messy. ("after the  semicolon, replace the word 'articles' with the letter 'i' and then change...")

It was an excellent interview experience with interesting problems, though the test cases for the second problem were bugged.

The IDE was extremely slow and unresponsive for the candidate. Frankly, it was an absolutely terrible experience. The candidate had to click every button multiple times, wait few seconds at a time for pop-ups to appear (if they appeared at all), every code change took 10-20 seconds to process and update the compiler errors/warnings, etc. We wasted a huge amount of time just fighting with the IDE and it was hard to stay focused on the task at hand. The environment kept complaining that both my and the candidate's connections were weak, which is simply not true because everything else works perfectly well. Besides, I would argue you don't need huge bandwidth to pass some text between two peers. If there are some background processes creating bottlenecks on the network, they need to be optimized. HackerRank needs to invest into stabilizing this experience. In its current state, it is almost unusable.

This platform failed, and it directly cost me a job opportunity.

The live coding environment was unusable. Keystrokes were dropped, characters appeared incorrectly, input lag was severe, and basic syntax could not be typed reliably. This was not user error. I tested multiple keyboards including the laptop keyboard, a wired USB keyboard, and a Bluetooth keyboard. The problem persisted across all of them.

Because the tool was broken, I was unable to demonstrate even basic coding ability. Instead of acknowledging or troubleshooting the issue, the interviewers defaulted to “it’s on your end” and ended the interview. No reset. No alternative. No attempt to verify the tool. I was disqualified due to a technical failure of HackerRank.

This is an absurd way to evaluate candidates, especially for senior and management level roles. I attempted to ask clarifying questions before starting, which is standard engineering behavior, and was told to just read the prompt. There was zero collaboration, zero discussion, and zero consideration for real world problem solving. The entire process was reduced to whether I could fight a broken text editor under time pressure.

A hiring decision was made based on tool malfunction rather than skill. That is unacceptable. If your platform cannot guarantee reliable input during live interviews, it should not be used as a gatekeeper for employment.

A few things:
1. When starting diagram question for system design question, I cannot add the text for my own question! The only options are to use a template or an existing question. I already have the text. Why can't I paste it in? The candidate was waiting for this, and I am fumbling around with it -- finally just pasting it into the diagram itself. This is very poor experience.

2. When the candidate is writing text in the diagram, they are using the text box. But that text box is for naming things, so it's centered text. It doesn't work well at all for note taking, or descriptions. Even clicking to get to the text to add/edit it is cumbersome.

3.I cannot see what people are writing in text boxes until they press enter! Therefore for 5-10 minutes, I cannot see what they are even doing. All I can do is hope that they are writing what they are saying, and then by the time they are done, I see it all at once. This is very poor experience.

Please, please add a system design use-case for your beta testing. Please watch a real candidate go through this. Please watch an interviewer who is trying to ask their own question. I know the templates are great, and maybe I should use them, but this shouldn't block me in real-time.

Thanks!
